---
title: "Gaussian Mixture Model"
format: html
---

## Overview

Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) is a probabilistic clustering algorithm that models data as a mixture of K Gaussian distributions. Each observation gets a **soft assignment** — a probability of belonging to each cluster — rather than a hard label.

## Parameters

| Parameter | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| K | 3 | Number of Gaussian components (clusters) |
| Max Iterations | 300 | Maximum EM (Expectation-Maximization) iterations |
| Seed | (random) | Optional random seed for reproducible results |

## When to Use

- Clusters overlap and hard assignments would lose information
- You want probability estimates for cluster membership
- Clusters have elliptical shapes (different variances per axis)
- You want a more flexible model than K-Means

## Soft Assignments

Unlike K-Means and DBSCAN, GMM produces a probability for each cluster for every frame. For example, a frame might have 60% probability of belonging to Cluster 0 and 40% to Cluster 1.

## Output

GMM produces both hard assignments (highest probability) and soft probabilities:

- **Entity groups**: One putative group per cluster (hard assignment)
- **Interval series**: Contiguous runs of each cluster assignment
- **Probability series**: One `AnalogTimeSeries` per cluster containing per-frame membership probabilities (enable "Write probabilities" in the clustering panel)

## Tips

- GMM often gives better results than K-Means when clusters are not spherical
- The probability output is useful for identifying frames near cluster boundaries
- Enable z-score normalization if features have different scales
- Set a seed when comparing different K values
